Annual Exam - 2020

Code: 65/2/1 (set-1)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 15 printed pages.
  2. Code number given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  3. Please check that this question paper contains 36 questions.
  4. Please write down the Serial Number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answerbook during this period.

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:80

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper comprises four Sections A, B, C and D. This question paper carries 36 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Section A - Questions no. 1 to 20 comprises of 20 questions of 1 mark each.
  3. Section B - Questions no. 21 to 26 comprises of 6 questions of 2 marks each.
  4. Section C - Questions no. 27 to 32 comprises of 6 questions of 4 marks each.
  5. Section D - Questions no. 33 to 36 comprises of 4 questions of 6 marks each.
  6. There is no overall choice in the question paper. However, an internal choice has been provided in 3 questions of one mark, 2 questions of two marks, 2 questions of four marks and 2 questions of six marks. Only one of the choices in such questions have to be attempted.
  7. In addition to this, separate instructions are given with each section and question, wherever necessary.
  8. Use of calculators is not permitted.

SECTION A

Question numbers 1 to 20 carry 1 mark each.
Question numbers 1 to 10 are multiple choice type questions. Select the correct option.
1.

The relation R in the set {1, 2, 3} given by R = {(1, 2), (2, 1), (1, 1)} is:

a. symmetric and transitive, but not reflexive
b. reflexive and symmetric, but not transitive
c. symmetric, but neither reflexive nor transitive
d. an equivalence relation
2.

{tex}\tan ^{-1} 3+\tan ^{-1} \lambda=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3+\lambda}{1-3 \lambda}\right){/tex} is valid for what values of {tex}\lambda{/tex}?

a. {tex}\lambda \in\left(-\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right){/tex}
b. {tex}\lambda>\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
c. {tex}\lambda<\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
d. All real values of {tex}\lambda{/tex}
3.

If A is a non-singular square matrix of order 3 such that A2 = 3A, then value of |A| is

a. -3
b. 3
c. 9
d. 27
4.

The function f : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R given by f(x) = - |x - 1| is:

a. continuous as well as differentiable at x = 1
b. not continuous but differentiable at x = 1
c. continuous but not differentiable at x = 1
d. neither continuous nor differentiable at x = 1
5.

Let A = {1, 3, 5}. Then the number of equivalence relations in A containing (1, 3) is:

a. 1
b. 2
c. 3
d. 4
6.

The interval in which the function f is given by f(x) = x2e-x is strictly increasing, is:

a. (0, 2)
b. {tex}(-\infty, \infty){/tex}
c. {tex}(-\infty, 0){/tex}
d. {tex}(2, \infty){/tex}
7.

If {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|{/tex} = 4 and -3 {tex}\leq{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\leq{/tex} 2, then {tex}|\lambda\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|{/tex} lies in

a. [0, 12]
b. [2, 3]
c. [8, 12]
d. [-12, 8]
8.

The vectors {tex}3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex},  {tex}2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} are coplanar if the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is:

a. -2
b. 0
c. 2
d. Any real number
9.

The area of a triangle formed by vertices O, A, and B, where {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OA}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OB}}=-3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} is

a. {tex}3 \sqrt{5}{/tex} sq. units
b. {tex}5 \sqrt{5}{/tex}  sq. units
c. {tex}6 \sqrt{5}{/tex} sq. units
d. 4 sq. units
10.

The coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point (2, - 3, 4) on the y-axis is:

a. (2, 3, 4)
b. (-2, -3, -4)
c. (0, -3, 0)
d. (2, 0, 4)

Fill in the blanks in question numbers 11 to 15.
11.

The range of the principal value branch of the function y = sec-1 x is ________

OR

The principal value of {tex}\cos^{-1}\left(-\frac12\right){/tex} is ________.
12.

Given a skew-symmetric matrix {tex}A = \left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & a & 1 \\ -1 & b & 1 \\ -1 & c & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, is ________.
13.

The distance between parallel planes 2x + y - 2z - 6 = 0 and 4x + 2y - 4z = 0 is ________ units.

OR

If P(1, 0, -3) is the foot of the perpendicular from the origin to the plane, then the cartesian equation of the plane is ________.
14.

If the radius of the circle is increasing at the rate of 0.5 cm/s, then the rate of increase of its circumference is ________.
15.

The corner points of the feasible region of an LPP are (0, 0), (0, 8), (2, 7), (5, 4) and (6, 0). The maximum profit P = 3x + 2y  occurs at the point ________.

Question numbers 16 to 20 are very short answer type questions.
16.

Differentiate sec2 (x2) with respect to x2.

OR

If y = f(x2) and f'(x) = {tex}e^{\sqrt{x}}{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex}
17.

Find the value of k, so that the function f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} \mathrm{kx}^2+5 & \text { if } & \mathrm{x} \leq 1 \\ 2 & \text { if } & \mathrm{x}>1 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 1.
18.

Evaluate: {tex}\int\limits_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \text x \cos ^{2} \text x {/tex} dx
19.

Find the general solution of the differential equation ey-x {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = 1.
20.

Find the coordinates of the point where the line {tex}\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+4}{7}=\frac{z+4}{2}{/tex} cuts the xy-plane.

SECTION B

Question numbers 21 to 26 carry 2 marks each.
21.

If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -3 & 2 \\ 1 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and I = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, find scalar k so that A2 + I = kA.
22.

If f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{\sec x-1}{\sec x+1}}{/tex}, find {tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex}

OR

Find f'(x) if f(x) = (tan x)tan x.
23.

Find: {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^{3} \text x}{\cos ^{3} \text x} \text {dx}{/tex}
24.

Find a vector {tex}\vec r{/tex} equally inclined to the three axes and whose magnitude is {tex}3 \sqrt 3{/tex} units.

OR

Find the angle between unit vectors {tex}\vec{\text a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\text b}{/tex} so that {tex}\sqrt{3} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} is also a unit vector.
25.

Find the points of intersection of the line {tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} and plane {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} = 5
26.

A purse contains 3 silver and 6 copper coins and a second purse contains 4 silver and 3 copper coins. If a coin is drawn at random from one of the two purses, find the probability that it is a silver coin.

SECTION C

Question numbers 27 to 32 carry 4 marks each.
27.

Check whether the relation R in the set N of natural numbers given by R = {(a, b): a is divisor of b} is reflexive, symmetric, or transitive. Also, determine whether R is an equivalence relation.

OR

Prove that {tex}\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{4}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{2}{9}=\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{5}\right){/tex}.
28.

If {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\log \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} {/tex}, prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex}.

OR

If y = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{a} \cos ^{-1} \mathrm{x}}{/tex}, -1 < x < 1, then show that
(1 - x2) {tex}\frac{\text d^2 \text y}{\text {dx}^2}-\text x \frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} - a2y = 0
29.

Find: {tex}\int \frac{\text x^3+1}{\text x^3-\text x} \text {dx}{/tex}
30.

Solve the following differential equation: {tex}\left(1+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\right) d y+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\left(1-\frac{y}{x}\right) dx{/tex} = 0
31.

Find the shortest distance between the lines
{tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{r}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}+\mu(4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex}
32.

A cottage industry manufactures pedestal lamps and wooden shades. Both the products require machine time as well as craftsman time in the making. The number of hour(s) required for producing 1 unit of each and the corresponding profit is given in the following table:

Item Machine Time Craftsman time Profit (in ₹)
Pedestal
lamp		 1·5 hours 3 hours 30
Wooden
shades		 3 hours 1 hour 20

In a day, the factory has availability of not more than 42 hours of machine time and 24 hours of craftsman time.
Assuming that all items manufactured are sold, how should the manufacturer schedule his daily production in order to maximise the profit? Formulate it as an LPP and solve it graphically.

SECTION D

Question numbers 33 to 36 carry 6 marks each.
33.

If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 4 \\ 2 & 3 & 5 \\ 5 & -2 & 6 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, find A-1 and use it to solve the following system of equations:
5x - y + 4z = 5
2x + 3y + 5z = 2
5x - 2y + 6z = - 1

OR

If x, y, z are different and {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x & x^{2} & 1+x^{3} \\ y & y^{2} & 1+y^{3} \\ z & z^{2} & 1+z^{3} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} then using properties of determinants show that 1 + xyz = 0.
34.

Amongst all open (from the top) right circular cylindrical boxes of volume {tex}125\pi{/tex} cm3, find the dimensions of the box which has the least surface area.
35.

Find the area of the region lying about x-axis and included between the circle x2 + y2 = 8x and inside of the parabola y2 = 4x.

OR

Using method of integration find the area of the triangle ABC, co-ordinates of whose vertices are A (2, 0), B (4, 5) and C (6, 3).
36.

Find the probability distribution of the random variable X, which denotes the number of doublets in four throws of a pair of dice. Hence, find the mean of the number of doublets (X).

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:80

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper comprises four Sections A, B, C and D. This question paper carries 36 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Section A - Questions no. 1 to 20 comprises of 20 questions of 1 mark each.
  3. Section B - Questions no. 21 to 26 comprises of 6 questions of 2 marks each.
  4. Section C - Questions no. 27 to 32 comprises of 6 questions of 4 marks each.
  5. Section D - Questions no. 33 to 36 comprises of 4 questions of 6 marks each.

  8. Use of calculators is not permitted.

SECTION A

Question numbers 1 to 20 carry 1 mark each.
Question numbers 1 to 10 are multiple choice type questions. Select the correct option.
1.

The relation R in the set {1, 2, 3} given by R = {(1, 2), (2, 1), (1, 1)} is:

a. symmetric and transitive, but not reflexive
b. reflexive and symmetric, but not transitive
c. symmetric, but neither reflexive nor transitive
d. an equivalence relation
Correct Answer: (a) symmetric and transitive, but not reflexive
Explanation:

A = {1, 2, 3}
R = {(1, 2), (2, 1), (1, 1)}
For a relation to be reflexive
(a, a) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} a {tex}\in{/tex} A
R does not contain (2, 2), (3, 3)
So relation 'R' is not reflexive
For a relation to be symmetric (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (b, a) {tex}\in{/tex} R
here, R contains (1, 2) and (2, 1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R is a symmetric relation
For a relation to be transitive (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (b, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R then (a, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R
here (1, 2) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (2, 1) {tex}\in{/tex} R
Also (1, 1) {tex}\in{/tex} R
So, R is transitive
As 'R' is not reflexive, R can not be equivalence relation.

2.

{tex}\tan ^{-1} 3+\tan ^{-1} \lambda=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3+\lambda}{1-3 \lambda}\right){/tex} is valid for what values of {tex}\lambda{/tex}?

a. {tex}\lambda \in\left(-\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right){/tex}
b. {tex}\lambda>\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
c. {tex}\lambda<\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
d. All real values of {tex}\lambda{/tex}
Correct Answer: (c) {tex}\lambda<\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
Explanation:

tan-1 x + tan-1 y = tan-1{tex}\left(\frac{x+y}{1-xy}\right){/tex} , For xy < 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} xy < 1
3{tex}\lambda{/tex} < 1
 {tex}\lambda<\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

 1
3.

If A is a non-singular square matrix of order 3 such that A2 = 3A, then value of |A| is

    If y = f(x2) and f'(x) = {tex}e^{\sqrt{x}}{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex}

    Solution:

    To find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} given that {tex}y=f\left(x^2\right){/tex} and {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=e^{\sqrt{x}}{/tex}, we can follow these steps:
    Step 1: Identify the function and its derivative
    We know that:
    {tex} -y=f\left(x^2\right){/tex}
    -The derivative of {tex}f{/tex} is given by {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=e^{\sqrt{x}}{/tex}
    Step 2: Apply the chain rule
    To find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}, we will use the chain rule. According to the chain rule:
    {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=f^{\prime}(u) \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex}
    where {tex}u=x^2{/tex}.
    Step 3: Differentiate {tex}u=x^2{/tex}
    Now, we differentiate {tex}\boldsymbol{u}{/tex} :
    {tex} \frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^2\right)=2 x{/tex}
    Step 4: Substitute {tex}u{/tex} into {tex}f^{\prime}{/tex}
    Next, we need to find {tex}f^{\prime}(u){/tex} where {tex}u=x^2{/tex} :
    {tex} f^{\prime}\left(x^2\right)=e^{\sqrt{x^2}}=e^x{/tex}
    (since {tex}\sqrt{x^2}=x{/tex} for {tex}x \geq 0{/tex} )
    Step 5: Combine the results
    Now we can substitute back into the equation from step 2:
    {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=f^{\prime}\left(x^2\right) \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}=e^x \cdot 2 x{/tex}
    Final Result
    Thus, we have:
    {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=2 x e^x{/tex}

    Find the coordinates of the point where the line {tex}\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+4}{7}=\frac{z+4}{2}{/tex} cuts the xy-plane.

    Solution:

    It is known that the equation of the line passing through the points {tex}\left(x_1, y_1, z_1\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(x_2, y_2, z_2\right){/tex} is
    {tex} \frac{x-x_1}{x_2-x_1}=\frac{y-y_1}{y_2-y_1}=\frac{z-z_1}{z_2-z_1}{/tex}
    So here we have,
    {tex} x_1=5, y_1=1, z_1=6 \text { and } x_2=3, y_2=4, z_2=1{/tex}
    The line passing through the points, {tex}(5,1,6){/tex} and {tex}(3,4,1){/tex} is given by,
    {tex} \frac{x-5}{3-5}=\frac{y-1}{4-1}=\frac{z-6}{1-6}=k {/tex}
    {tex} \text { Let } \frac{x-5}{-2}=\frac{y-1}{3}=\frac{z-6}{-5}=k {/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x-5}{-2}=k {/tex}
    {tex} \therefore x=5-2 k {/tex} ...(1)
    {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{y-1}{3}=k {/tex}
    {tex} \therefore y=3 k+1 {/tex} ...(2)
    {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{z-6}{-5}=k {/tex}
    {tex} \therefore z=6-5 k {/tex} ...(3)
    Any point on the line is of the form {tex}(5-2 k, 3 k+1,6-5 k){/tex}
    Since, the line passes through {tex}Y Z-{/tex} plane, then {tex}x=0{/tex}
    From (1)
    {tex} \Rightarrow 5-2 k=0 {/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow k=\frac{5}{2} {/tex}
    Substituting value of {tex}k{/tex} in (2) we get,
    {tex} \Rightarrow y=3 \times \frac{5}{2}+1 {/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow y=\frac{15+2}{2} {/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow y=\frac{17}{2} {/tex}
    Substituting value of {tex}k{/tex} in (3) we get,
    {tex} \Rightarrow z=6-5\left(\frac{5}{2}\right) {/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow z=6-\frac{25}{2} {/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow z=\frac{12-25}{2} {/tex}
    {tex} \therefore z=\frac{-13}{2} {/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The required point is {tex}\left(0, \frac{17}{2}, \frac{-13}{2}\right){/tex}

    23.

    Find: {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^{3} \text x}{\cos ^{3} \text x} \text {dx}{/tex}

    Solution:

    To solve the integral {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x{/tex}, we can first rewrite the expression in terms of simpler trigonometric functions.
    We know that:
    {tex} \tan x=\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}{/tex}
    Therefore,
    {tex} \tan ^3 x=\frac{\sin ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x}{/tex}
    Now, substitute this into the original integral:
    {tex} \int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x=\int \frac{\sin ^3 x}{\cos ^6 x} d x{/tex}
    We will now attempt to express this in a more solvable form using a substitution. Let:
    {tex} u=\cos x \Rightarrow d u=-\sin x d x{/tex}
    Thus, {tex}\sin x d x=-d u{/tex}, and the integral becomes:
    {tex} \int \frac{\left(1-u^2\right) u^3}{u^6}(-d u){/tex}
    Simplifying the expression inside the integral:
    {tex} =\int \frac{-\left(1-u^2\right)}{u^3} d u{/tex}
    Now separate the terms:
    {tex} =-\int\left(\frac{1}{u^3}-\frac{1}{u}\right) d u{/tex}
    Now we can integrate each term separately:
    {tex} =-\left(\int u^{-3} d u-\int u^{-1} d u\right){/tex}
    Using the standard integral formulas:
    {tex} \int u^{-3} d u=\frac{u^{-2}}{-2}=-\frac{1}{2 u^2}, \int u^{-1} d u=\ln |u|{/tex}
    Thus, the result becomes:
    {tex} =-\left(-\frac{1}{2 u^2}-\ln |u|\right){/tex}
    Substitute back {tex}u=\cos x{/tex} :
    {tex} =\frac{1}{2 \cos ^2 x}+\ln |\cos x|+C{/tex}
    Finally, we have the solution to the integral:
    {tex} \int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x=\frac{1}{2 \cos ^2 x}+\ln |\cos x|+C{/tex}

    26.

    A purse contains 3 silver and 6 copper coins and a second purse contains 4 silver and 3 copper coins. If a coin is drawn at random from one of the two purses, find the probability that it is a silver coin.

    Solution:

    {tex}E_1{/tex} : Event of coin drawn from purse 1
    {tex}{E}_2{/tex} : Event of coin drawn from purse 2
    A : Silver coin is drawn
    {tex} {P}\left({E}_1\right)=\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
    {tex} {P}\left({E}_2\right)=\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
    {tex} {P}\left(\frac{{~A}}{{E}_1}\right)=\frac{3}{9} {/tex}
    {tex} {P}\left(\frac{{~A}}{{E}_2}\right)=\frac{4}{7} {/tex}

    27.

    Check whether the relation R in the set N of natural numbers given by R = {(a, b): a is divisor of b} is reflexive, symmetric, or transitive. Also, determine whether R is an equivalence relation.

    Solution:

    Check for Reflexive:

    If (a,a) for every a {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is Reflexive

    Here a divided a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a,a) {tex}\in{/tex} R

    R is Reflexive

    Check for Symmetric

    If (a,b) {tex}\in{/tex} R then (b,a) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is symmetric

    Here a divided by b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1,2) {tex}\in{/tex} but (2,1) {tex}\not\in{/tex}​​​​​​​ R

    R is not symmetric

    Check for transitive

    If (a,b), (b,c) {tex}\in{/tex} R then (a,c) {tex}\in{/tex}​​​​​​​ R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is transitive

    Here a divides b and b divides c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a divides c (a,c) {tex}\in{/tex}​​​​​​​ R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is transitive

    As R is not symmetric. Hence it is not an equivalence relation.

    Solution:

    30.

    Solve the following differential equation: {tex}\left(1+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\right) d y+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\left(1-\frac{y}{x}\right) dx{/tex} = 0

    Solution:

    {tex}\left(1+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\right) d y+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\left(1-\frac{y}{x}\right) dx=0{/tex}
    {tex}dy={\textstyle\frac{-e^\frac yx\left(1-\frac yx\right)dx}{\left(1+e^\frac yx\right)}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}={\textstyle\frac{e^\frac yx\left(\frac yx-1\right)}{\left(1+e^\frac yx\right)}}{/tex}
    As it is form of homogeneous equation
    Put y = vx, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=v+x\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex}
    {tex}v+x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{e^\frac{vx}x\left(\frac{vx}x-1\right)}{\left(1+e^\frac{vx}x\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}v+x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{e^v\left(v-1\right)}{\left(1+e^v\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{v.e^v-e^v}{\left(1+e^v\right)}-v{/tex}
    {tex}x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{v.e^v-e^v-v-v.e^v}{\left(1+e^v\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{-\left(e^v+v\right)}{\left(1+e^v\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}\int\frac{1+e^v}{v+e^v}dv=-\int\frac{dx}x{/tex}
    {tex}\log\left|e^v+v\right|=-\log x+\log C{/tex}
    {tex}e^v+v=\frac Cx{/tex}
    {tex}e^{\textstyle\frac yx}+\frac yx=\frac Cx{/tex}
    {tex}xe^{\textstyle\frac yx}+y=C{/tex}

    Solution:

    {tex}\Delta = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{{x^2}}&1 \\ y&{{y^2}}&1 \\ z&{{z^2}}&1 \end{array}} \right| +\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{{x^2}}&{{x^3}} \\ y&{{y^2}}&{{y^3}} \\ z&{{z^2}}&{{z^3}} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} 
    Applying {tex}C_1\leftrightarrow C_3{/tex} and then {tex}C_3\leftrightarrow C_2{/tex} in the first determinant and taking x,y,z common from R1, R2, R3 respectively in the second determinant
    {tex}= \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&{{x^2}} \\ 1&y&{{y^2}} \\ 1&z&{{z^2}} \end{array}} \right| + {xyz} \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&{{x^2}} \\ 1&y&{{y^2}} \\ 1&z&{{z^2}} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} 
    {tex}= \left( {1 + xyz} \right)\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&{{x^2}} \\ 1&y&{{y^2}} \\ 1&z&{{z^2}} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} 
    Applying {tex}R_2\rightarrow R_2-R_1 \ and\ R_3\rightarrow R_3-R_1{/tex} 
    {tex}= \left( {1 + xyz} \right)\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&{{x^2}} \\ 0&{y - x}&{{y^2} - {x^2}} \\ 0&{z - x}&{{z^2} - {x^2}} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} 
    {tex} = \left( {1 + xyz} \right)\left( {y - x} \right)\left( {z - x} \right)\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&{{x^2}} \\ 0&1&{y + x} \\ 0&1&{z + x} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} 
    = (1 + xyz)(y - x)(z - x)(z - y)
    {tex}\Delta {/tex} = 0 (Given)
    x, y, z all are different
    {tex}x - y \ne 0{/tex} ,y - z = 0, {tex}z - x \ne 0{/tex} 
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} 1 + xyz = 0.

    Which is the required solution.

    35.

    Find the area of the region lying about x-axis and included between the circle x2 + y2 = 8x and inside of the parabola y2 = 4x.

    Solution:

    Given, equation of circle is x2 + y2 = 8x
    (x - 4)2 + y2 = 16 ...(i)
    Centre is (4,0) and radius is 4 and equation of parabola is y2 = 4x ...(ii)
    Let required area = I
    {tex}\therefore \mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{4} y \ of \ the \ parabola \ d x \ =\int_{4}^{8} y \ of \ the \ circle \ d x{/tex}
    {tex}=\int_{0}^{4} 2 \sqrt{x} d x+\int_{4}^{8} \sqrt{4^{2}-(x-4)^{2}} d x{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{I}_{1}=2 \int_{0}^{4} \sqrt{x} d x{/tex}
    {tex}I_{1}=2\left[\frac{x^{3 / 2}}{3 / 2}\right]_{0}^{4}{/tex}

    {tex}I_{1}=\frac{4}{3}\left[4^{\frac{3}{2}}-0\right]{/tex}
    {tex}I_{1}=\frac{4}{3}(8)=\frac{32}{3}{/tex}
    and {tex}\mathrm{I}_{2}=\int_{4}^{8} \sqrt{4^{2}-(x-4)^{2}} d x{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{I}_{2}=\left[\frac{x-4}{2} \sqrt{4^{2}-(x-4)^{2}}+\frac{16}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x-4}{4}\right)\right]_{4}^{8}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{I}_{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{4}{2}(0)+16 \sin ^{-1}(1)\right)-(0)\right]{/tex}
    {tex}I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 16 \frac{\pi}{2}=4 \pi{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\left(\frac{32}{3}+4 \pi\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{4}{3}(8+3 \pi){/tex} sq. units

    Using method of integration find the area of the triangle ABC, co-ordinates of whose vertices are A (2, 0), B (4, 5) and C (6, 3).

    Solution:

    Points in the form of line in the given diagram

    {tex}{/tex}The equation of side AB is,
    {tex}y-0=\frac{5-0}{4-2}(x-2){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{5}{2}(x-2){/tex}
    The equation of side BC is,

    {tex}\Rightarrow y-3=-1(x-6){/tex}

    {tex}y-0=\frac{3-0}{6-2}(x-2){/tex}

    Area {tex} = \frac{5}{2}\int_2^4 {(x - 2)dx + \int_4^6 { - (x - 9)dx - \frac{3}{4}\int_2^6 {(x - 2)dx} } } {/tex}
    {tex}A = \int_2^4 {\frac{5}{2}(x - 2)dx + \int_0^1 { - (x + 9)dx + \int_6^2 {\frac{3}{4}(x - 2)dx} } } {/tex}
    On integrating we get,
    {tex}A = \frac{5}{2}\left[ {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} - 2x} \right]_2^4 + \left[ {\frac{{ - {x^2}}}{2} + 9x} \right]_1^0 - \frac{3}{4}\left[ {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} - 2x} \right]_2^6{/tex}
    On applying limits we get,

    {tex}A = 5 - 8 - \frac{3}{4}(8){/tex}

    36.

    Find the probability distribution of the random variable X, which denotes the number of doublets in four throws of a pair of dice. Hence, find the mean of the number of doublets (X).

    Solution:

    Let p denote the probability of getting a doublet in a single throw of a pair of dice. Then
    {tex}p=\frac{6}{36}=\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
    Therefore. q = 1 - p = 1 - {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex}
    Let X denote the number of doublets in four throws of a pair of dice. Clearly, X has the binomial
    n = 4, p = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} and q = {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(X = r) = {tex}{ }^{4} C_{r}\left(\frac{5}{6}\right)^{4-r}\left(\frac{1}{6}\right){/tex} where r = 0, 1, 2, 3 and 4
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(X = r) = {tex}^4C_{r} \frac{5^{4-r}}{6^{4}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(0 success)
    = P(X = 0)
    {tex}={ }^{4} C_{0} \cdot \frac{5^{4-0}}{6^{4}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{5^{4}}{6^{4}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{625}{1296}{/tex}
    P(1 success)
    = P(X = 1)
    = {tex}{ }^{4} C_{1} \cdot \frac{5^{4-1}}{6^{4}}{/tex}
    = {tex}4 \cdot \frac{5^{3}}{6^{4}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{125}{324}{/tex}
    P(2 sucesses)
    = P(X = 2)
    = {tex}^4C_{2} \frac{5^{4-2}}{6^{4}}{/tex}
    {tex}=6 \frac{25}{1296}{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac{25}{216 }{/tex}
    P(3 successes)
    = P(X = 3)
    {tex}={ }^{4} C_{3} \cdot \frac{5^{4-3}}{6^{4}}{/tex}
    {tex}=4 \cdot \frac{5}{6^{4}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{5}{324}{/tex}
    P(4 successes)
    P(X = 4)
    {tex}={ }^{4} C_{4} \cdot \frac{5^{4-4}}{6^{4}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{1296}{/tex}
    Thus, the probability distribution of X is given by
    {tex}0 \frac{625}{1296}{/tex}, {tex}\text { 1 } \frac{125}{324}{/tex}, {tex}2 \frac{25}{216}{/tex}, {tex}3 \frac{5}{324}{/tex}, {tex}4 \frac{1}{1296}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Mean of X
    {tex}=\sum x_{i} p_{i}{/tex}
    {tex}=0 \times \frac{625}{1296}+1 \times \frac{125}{324}+2 \times \frac{25}{216}+3 \times \frac{5}{324}+4 \times \frac{1}{1296}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{125}{324}+\frac{50}{216}+\frac{15}{324}+\frac{1}{324}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
    Now,
    {tex}\sum x_{i}^{2} p_{i}{/tex} = {tex}0^{2} \times \frac{625}{1296}+1^{2} \times \frac{125}{324}+2^{2} \times \frac{25}{216}+3^{2} \times \frac{5}{324}+4^{2} \times \frac{1}{1296}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{125}{324}+\frac{100}{216}+\frac{45}{324}+\frac{4}{324}{/tex}
    = 1
    {tex}\therefore \operatorname{Var}(X)=\sum x_{i}^{2} p_{i}-\left(\sum x_{i} p_{i}\right)^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=1-\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
    Thus, the mean and the variance of the distribution are
    {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex}, respectively.

Find: {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^{3} \text x}{\cos ^{3} \text x} \text {dx}{/tex} Solution: To solve the integral {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x{/tex} , we can first rewrite the expression in terms of simpler trigonometric functions. We know that: {tex} \tan x=\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}{/tex} Therefore, {tex} \tan ^3 x=\frac{\sin ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x}{/tex} Now, substitute this into the original integral: {tex} \int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x=\int \frac{\sin ^3 x}{\cos ^6 x} d x{/tex} We will now attempt to express this in a more solvable form using a substitution. Let: {tex} u=\cos x \Rightarrow d u=-\sin x d x{/tex} Thus, {tex}\sin x d x=-d u{/tex} , and the integral becomes: {tex} \int \frac{\left(1-u^2\right) u^3}{u^6}(-d u){/tex} Simplifying the expression inside the integral: {tex} =\int \frac{-\left(1-u^2\right)}{u^3} d u{/tex} Now separate the terms: {tex} =-\int\left(\frac{1}{u^3}-\frac{1}{u}\right) d u{/tex} Now we can integrate each term separately: {tex} =-\left(\int u^{-3} d u-\int u^{-1} d u\right){/tex} Using the standard integral formulas: {tex} \int u^{-3} d u=\frac{u^{-2}}{-2}=-\frac{1}{2 u^2}, \int u^{-1} d u=\ln |u|{/tex} Thus, the result becomes: {tex} =-\left(-\frac{1}{2 u^2}-\ln |u|\right){/tex} Substitute back {tex}u=\cos x{/tex} : {tex} =\frac{1}{2 \cos ^2 x}+\ln |\cos x|+C{/tex} Finally, we have the solution to the integral: {tex} \int \frac{\tan ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x} d x=\frac{1}{2 \cos ^2 x}+\ln |\cos x|+C{/tex} 2 24. Solve the following differential equation: {tex}\left(1+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\right) d y+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\left(1-\frac{y}{x}\right) dx{/tex} = 0 Solution: {tex}\left(1+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\right) d y+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\left(1-\frac{y}{x}\right) dx=0{/tex} {tex}dy={\textstyle\frac{-e^\frac yx\left(1-\frac yx\right)dx}{\left(1+e^\frac yx\right)}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}={\textstyle\frac{e^\frac yx\left(\frac yx-1\right)}{\left(1+e^\frac yx\right)}}{/tex} As it is form of homogeneous equation Put y = vx, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=v+x\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} {tex}v+x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{e^\frac{vx}x\left(\frac{vx}x-1\right)}{\left(1+e^\frac{vx}x\right)}{/tex} {tex}v+x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{e^v\left(v-1\right)}{\left(1+e^v\right)}{/tex} {tex}x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{v.e^v-e^v}{\left(1+e^v\right)}-v{/tex} {tex}x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{v.e^v-e^v-v-v.e^v}{\left(1+e^v\right)}{/tex} {tex}x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{-\left(e^v+v\right)}{\left(1+e^v\right)}{/tex} {tex}\int\frac{1+e^v}{v+e^v}dv=-\int\frac{dx}x{/tex} {tex}\log\left|e^v+v\right|=-\log x+\log C{/tex} {tex}e^v+v=\frac Cx{/tex} {tex}e^{\textstyle\frac yx}+\frac yx=\frac Cx{/tex} {tex}xe^{\textstyle\frac yx}+y=C{/tex} 4 31. If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 4 \\ 2 & 3 & 5 \\ 5 & -2 & 6 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , find A -1 and use it to solve the following system of equations: 5x - y + 4z = 5 2x + 3y + 5z = 2 5x - 2y + 6z = - 1 Solution: {tex} |{A}|=51 {/tex} {tex} \text { Cofactors: } {/tex} {tex} {A}_{11}=28 {~A}_{12}=13 {~A}_{13}=-19 {/tex} {tex} {~A}_{21}=-2 {~A}_{22}=10 {~A}_{23}=5 {/tex} {tex} {~A}_{31}=-17 {~A}_{32}=-17 {~A}_{33}=17 {/tex} {tex} {A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{51}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 28 & -2 & -17 \\ 13 & 10 & -17 \\ -19 & 5 & 17 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\text { Given system is } {AX}={B} \text { where } {X}=\left[\begin{array}{l} {x} \\ {y} \\ {z} \end{array}\right] {B}=\left[\begin{array}{r} 5 \\ 2 \\ -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {X}={A}^{-1} {~B}=\frac{1}{51}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 28 & -2 & -17 \\ 13 & 10 & -17 \\ -19 & 5 & 17 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{r} 5 \\ 2 \\ -1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{r} 3 \\ 2 \\ -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}{x}=3, {y}=2, {z}=-2{/tex} 6 OR If x, y, z are different and {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x & x^{2} & 1+x^{3} \\ y & y^{2} & 1+y^{3} \\ z & z^{2} & 1+z^{3} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} then using properties of determinants show that 1 + xyz = 0. Solution: {tex}\Delta = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{{x^2}}&1 \\ y&{{y^2}}&1 \\ z&{{z^2}}&1 \end{array}} \right| +\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{{x^2}}&{{x^3}} \\ y&{{y^2}}&{{y^3}} \\ z&{{z^2}}&{{z^3}} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} Applying {tex}C_1\leftrightarrow C_3{/tex} and then {tex}C_3\leftrightarrow C_2{/tex} in the first determinant and taking x,y,z common from R 1 , R 2 , R 3 respectively in the second determinant {tex}= \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&{{x^2}} \\ 1&y&{{y^2}} \\ 1&z&{{z^2}} \end{array}} \right| + {xyz} \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&{{x^2}} \\ 1&y&{{y^2}} \\ 1&z&{{z^2}} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left( {1 + xyz} \right)\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&{{x^2}} \\ 1&y&{{y^2}} \\ 1&z&{{z^2}} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} Applying {tex}R_2\rightarrow R_2-R_1 \ and\ R_3\rightarrow R_3-R_1{/tex} {tex}= \left( {1 + xyz} \right)\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&{{x^2}} \\ 0&{y - x}&{{y^2} - {x^2}} \\ 0&{z - x}&{{z^2} - {x^2}} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left( {1 + xyz} \right)\left( {y - x} \right)\left( {z - x} \right)\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&{{x^2}} \\ 0&1&{y + x} \\ 0&1&{z + x} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} = (1 + xyz)(y - x)(z - x)(z - y) {tex}\Delta {/tex} = 0 (Given) x, y, z all are different {tex}x - y \ne 0{/tex} ,y - z = 0, {tex}z - x \ne 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} 1 + xyz = 0. Solution: Given, equation of circle is x 2 + y 2 = 8x (x - 4) 2 + y 2 = 16 ...(i) Centre is (4,0) and radius is 4 and equation of parabola is y 2 = 4x ...(ii) Let required area = I {tex}\therefore \mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{4} y \ of \ the \ parabola \ d x \ =\int_{4}^{8} y \ of \ the \ circle \ d x{/tex} {tex}=\int_{0}^{4} 2 \sqrt{x} d x+\int_{4}^{8} \sqrt{4^{2}-(x-4)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{I}_{1}=2 \int_{0}^{4} \sqrt{x} d x{/tex} {tex}I_{1}=2\left[\frac{x^{3 / 2}}{3 / 2}\right]_{0}^{4}{/tex} {tex}I_{1}=\frac{4}{3}\left[4^{\frac{3}{2}}-0\right]{/tex} {tex}I_{1}=\frac{4}{3}(8)=\frac{32}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{I}_{2}=\int_{4}^{8} \sqrt{4^{2}-(x-4)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{I}_{2}=\left[\frac{x-4}{2} \sqrt{4^{2}-(x-4)^{2}}+\frac{16}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x-4}{4}\right)\right]_{4}^{8}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{I}_{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{4}{2}(0)+16 \sin ^{-1}(1)\right)-(0)\right]{/tex} {tex}I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 16 \frac{\pi}{2}=4 \pi{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{32}{3}+4 \pi\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{4}{3}(8+3 \pi){/tex} sq. units 6 OR Using method of integration find the area of the triangle ABC, co-ordinates of whose vertices are A (2, 0), B (4, 5) and C (6, 3).
